speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about the mandatory year of housekeeping work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly. It's like I said. We had to all do once a year. And my parents liked those folks over there in the house, because she thought they were pretty [NON-ENGLISH] people that have a daughter, teached her the house-- cleaning the house and how the proper way to set the table and how to wash the clothes and boil the things, and how to learn how to cook. Not just what we learned from her, she wanted us to learn from somebody else, other people lived. So this is what we had to do for a year. And like I said, my sisters never told. They all had a wonderful time. Well, I found out because you have a room way upstairs in the top floor, like an attic type of a thing. And it comes down like an A-frame type of thing. So you have a little room there. And there was a [INAUDIBLE] by the bell that they had down from the bedroom, from their bedroom, those two old folks. As a matter of fact, they had a son and a daughter. And they were both military, already when I started there. I think the daughter was some kind of a captain or something. And they were military people too. And they had a bell down with the old folks that I had to ring the bell up there. It's 5 o'clock in the morning, you almost fell out of the bed. But then I found out that old man was creeping up those stairs. And I got scared. So the door was locked, sure, but that old man had a key. So what I did, I put the chair underneath that thing. I was frightened, 14-year-old girl. And I told my mother, and my mother didn't believe me. My mother said, ah, never mind. You just want to get out of it. You want to get out of that work. I said, no Mama. So then I told my sisters, and I told my sisters that he was trying to come in my room. Then my sisters convinced my mother, saying, Mom, he did the same thing to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he actually molest you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he never got in. He never came in because I put that thing up. But then I had to have for my two sisters had to say the OK on that. So that's when she took me out of there. So I never finished my year.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I probably was there for six months. I had to take care on weekends-- there was an old woman, she was crippled. I have to take her to the movies. And I waited on her, you know. For a matter of fact, the first time I seen a pair of false teeth. Took the false teeth out of her mouth at night, and I had to put them in the water. That's a terrible thing for a 14-year-old girl. Well, I had to do that. And of course, they had a garden. I'd do garden work, cooking, breakfast, supper. Just ring the bell when they were ready to come in. I'll never forget, the old man had his beard, like this old man from the mountain. And when we had soup, he had that soup. He put the serviette, you know, the napkin, there. But it always got into the beard of his. And I think it was a horrible picture to see, for such a young girl. I didn't like it. But that's the situation. And I was very unhappy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in my hometown. But see, my other sisters didn't say anything. They didn't say nothing to my mother, they didn't. And I had told her from the beginning, Mom, I don't want to go over there. I don't need that. And she says, well, you're going to, you have to. Otherwise, I've got to put you somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wanted to go-- the other sister was in that bakery, remember? That's the one I really wanted to go. I wanted to work with the animals there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, well, I have to put you over there. She said, now you already have six months. You finish up over there. When she found out from my sisters that it was really so, then she went up there and she confronted them. She said, you know, you're scaring the young girls, climbing up there at night. I told my mom, I don't know how the heck that old man got up there. Must have been at least 69, 67. Three, four, flights high. He had a blue room, a green room, and a yellow room, a smoke room. Had 13 room in that house. But he did. He came up there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had the feeling. You had kind of feeling, being even 14, I noticed sometimes, wiping, kind of [INAUDIBLE] there, you know what I mean? The first few times, I didn't think nothing of it. Then it got a little more. So that's when I got the idea, don't trust that one. You know, we were very partial anyway, talking to our parents that way. We weren't raised-- Somehow we got-- I don't know if I should even mention this-- but our periods and stuff, you know I mean? My mother didn't go into all this big situation. It was from sister to sister. If you got your-- even it would be a shame from sometimes with the sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you got the period and didn't know who to talk to, so you talk to the older sister. And then she explained it very simply. It was very easy. It's something we all get, and we have to. And of course, those days we didn't have our Kotex. You could go to the store today and get all those nice things. Those days you had to knit yourself your own little ones. And of course I already knew about that, because I seen them in the wash. Hanging on the line all the time, maybe one day it was [INAUDIBLE] in there. And that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here, just to say this, we were not raised to a little bit-- what do you call that, modesty, whatever it is. That's the way we were raised. But of course, as we've gotten older, then between the girls and we learned all we had to learn. Mother didn't have to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll never forget our father. Father put us all together, it was about four or five of us. And he said, I want to sit you down and I'm going to have a talk with you girls, because you're getting big enough for all this. And that I'm going to tell you one time, and I'm never going to tell you anything again. Instead of her saying this, he-- because she was there in the kitchen. We had a long, custom-made table, you could just imagine, with a bench like I have there but a long one. And then there was chairs in the front for the bigger kids. And he on one end, and she on the other end. All the little ones in the back on the benches.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we were all sitting and wonder what he was coming up with. So he was giving us a lesson about sex and the whole thing. I'm just about 14, 13 or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'm going to tell it to you once. And I'll never say it again. If any one of you-- he didn't even say the word-- if any one of you girls, he just made the sign like that, are that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had two steps in front of the house. You see those two steps? Just forget about what those steps are. And I don't say no more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was the whole sex act explanation we got from both of them. Of course, none of us never got in any kind of trouble. Not that they didn't have those things those days. They had them too, but that's what our sex education. So we never really sat down with the mama.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- Well, my mother was 16 when she would get married. Where did she get her sex education? She had to learn herself too. And that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was wonderful, really. There was no mistakes made. There was nothing. All this need the sex education nowadays. There's more trouble today, thank God has got the liver pills.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, you were a young girl when the Nazis came to power in Germany, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was a very young girl, in 1938, '36 they started. My God, I was 10, 11 years old when this all started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of it? Did it change your life any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this time, no, not at that time. Because they were starting with the Hitler Jugend and BDM, those little clubs there you joined. And I wanted to be in that BDM too. My girlfriends were all in the, had the cute little blue skirts, with their little mustard-colored blouses with their little neck. They used to have little meetings. And I thought it would be nice to get away from the housework a little bit, go with that girlfriend. My mother says, yeah, I give you BDM. You've got plenty of it right here to do. So we were never allowed to join anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because your mom wouldn't let you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wouldn't let us. For some reason-- my father wouldn't either. So anyway, and there was no big situation. This had all just got started. And I will have to tell you, war broke out. Even the two of my brothers were-- my brother Louis, who was sent after that into the concentration camp, he was drafted in the service. They had drafted him in the service. And after they started, all the 100% Jewish people were already gone. I was arrested in 19-- We were arrested in 1943. So most of the Jewish people already were gone. Now they started to check the bloodlines. That's how the category we got into. When they found out about the bloodline, they sent my brother Louis home from the service.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he go into the service? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in '39 or in '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it sounds like you were treated as good Germans until they checked the bloodlines.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about checking the bloodlines. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember-- like I say, I was 14. And we all had jobs. They had jobs, because the war was there, you see. And there was no one allowed-- they didn't give us the permit to go traveling out with the wagons. So you had to always have the paper signed from the school. So we didn't get none.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my dad had to be-- That was finished now. Now we couldn't travel anymore. No more traveling in the summertime?

speaker: interviewee
text: No more traveling in the summertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: From '39 on?

speaker: interviewee
text: From about '39, '40, early '40. That was the end. So we all had jobs. One of them was in the knitting mill. My sister Lena, who came home, after about three weeks she died. She was in a knitting mill. My sister was working in the farm. I was working in the-- I was only a young girl, but I was already working in the little factory there. We were doing some wooden items we were making there. And then my dad used to bring me little lunches.

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah, because I was so young. Which is cruel in that, and like the rest of the war, it was getting going. And soldiers were getting drafted and all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no reason. As a matter of fact, we had no reason even to have a fear. Because we didn't know anything. Until this one particular morning in March. And it was March 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. Early in the morning, one of my sisters-- my sister Lena already left for the mill. She had to go on a bike, had about six kilometers to go. And my other sister already left, my sister Mathilde. Somebody knocked on the door. And it was the burgermeister from [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what a burgermeister is.

speaker: interviewee
text: The burgermeister is the mayor.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So he knocked on the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: He knocked at the door. And my mother first went up, early, 5:30. And he said, Mrs. Backer, is your husband up?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said no. She says, but I could get him. Is something wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, well, why don't you go get your husband up. Because the little ones, smaller ones were still in bed. And so when he came out in the kitchen, let him in the kitchen. And he said they were [NON-ENGLISH], Louis. They called him by first name, they knew each other. He said, I got some authorities out here that your house, they surrounded your house. We got some orders. They have to bring your family in. They want to take you to Frankfurt. There's something-- he said, don't get upset. There's something about the family tree. They're checking your family tree. And it's probably be gone for about three or four days.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said, oh God, my kids aren't all here. My daughter is out. He said, I picked her up. She's on the way back here. So there, and when my dad, he said, well what about the animals? He said it's all taken care of. So he said just pack a little something for three days, he said to my mother, for the kids, whatever you need. And he said that's all you really need. So honestly, as honestly can be, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother packed a couple of suitcases and got us dressed and said, you, take your jacket, make sure the little one has got this and that and this and that. Fritz at that time was four years old, my youngest brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you couldn't go no place. They had ever so many [GERMAN]. They were the SS. They had a [INAUDIBLE], like big criminals we were or something. And of course all the neighbors, they didn't know either what was going on. Our neighbor kids, people we grew up with, children my own age, my friends. And of course, we didn't know. We thought we'd be back. We didn't even make such a big commotion. Because my dad said, well, that has to be done. We have to do whatever needs. He didn't know what it was all about. And sure enough, we went down on that railroad there and the depot. And there was a passenger car. But the passenger car was empty. It was for us. We were all in there. And my mother was a little upset and nervous, you know, those kids, and the kids were small. And my dad says, oh, not to be worried about. There's nothing to worry about. But for a matter of fact, I tell you, they didn't know about concentration-- they knew about [GERMAN]. There were [GERMAN] where they put the people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us what that is.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN], people who didn't want to work. Even before the war, the government-- money for the children. They pay for the-- support the child. They called them [GERMAN]. And they made them go for six months or a year in those work camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's all we knew about. But Auschwitz, there was not on the map. It was there, but they didn't publicize this, you see? That's how little backwards my parents were. The Nazi soldiers that were there, were they from out of town or did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were not from this little Biedenkopf. They came from some military thing. If you-- later on in life, we thought about it, of course. No, Biedenkopf was just a little sleepy town. I mean, [INAUDIBLE] was never bombed. No big factories of any kind, just all homemade, little stuff. So there was not bombed at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, when we got to Frankfurt with that single wagon. How long a drive was it? Ride was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frankfurt is 110 kilometers from my hometown. So as we moved there, we got changed around a little bit with that wagon, until we got to Frankfurt. And it was late in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one of the Nazis came on the door and said, well, pack all your little things. We're going to get out here. And we have to stop here. Tomorrow morning you'll be able to talk to someone. My dad said, well, what's going on? Why can't I talk to somebody here? I mean, what's going on here? So he said, well, that's all I have to say. We have to get out. We have to bring you over there. It was like some thing they had, where they collected all the some of the person, some of the Jewish people, Gypsy people. They had them together till that big-- we didn't know it at that time. But that's what it was. So of course we had to get out and walk into this big hall. And there was like stalls in there. And it was full with people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when my dad found out from Jewish people, OK, whatever was there that was going on, what was going on. And that we were going to go to Auschwitz. And he heard about Auschwitz. And my dad says, oh, that's not us. Oh, no, no, we got to go. Tomorrow morning, I'm going to see the captain. And they're going to take us here to the-- never came. Next morning, that big train came and was already wagon full. And that's what they picked up, what was in there. And my mother knew then already. My mother said, hold hands. Stay together. Hold hands. Carry the small ones. All us stick together, so we don't get lost. We get into that one wagon. There's so many people there put in. And no pee pee, there is no nothing. They had some straw in the corner. You had to do your thing there. Anyway, we didn't get out of there for three days. We were in there for three days. Night and day and occasionally in big cities. I guess we had to stop, till another train, we were connected again and connected again. Until on the end, that whole train was just full of prisoners, [GERMAN], what we call them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people, Gypsy people, Polish people, political people. All kinds in the different wagons. And as far as the toiletry was concerned, I forget to tell, you there was straw in the thing. And here is 14-year-old girls, 15-year-old girls, young women, and wives. And the men were so nice. Made little circles with their backs, turned the backs, and we could do our little business there. The men themselves were very nice. All strange people, you don't never met. And they got to talking. And there had a rumor here, and there was a rumor there. And everybody heard something. And it all melted down to Auschwitz. Well, three days later, we did stop in Auschwitz. Creak! With those boxcars or whatever you call them now. And my mom was petrified. You know, when you're young, you're not quite that petrified. We didn't expect all this. Even if you told it to me then, it wouldn't have affected me like that, until you actually get into the situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you thinking? You were 14. What did you think was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: 14. I was more concerned about the little ones. That [GERMAN], and taking like mothers that stay together. We don't want to get lost, we don't want to get lost. There's so many people there, we're going to get separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, we hang together. It's all we were concentrating on, staying together. And we got there at night. Did you hold hands all three days?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] It was awful. They got there at night, when I tell you. And all those bright lights. Lights from everywhere and dust. And this way and that way. Some of them went this way, some of them went that way in the nighttime. And of course from there on, they put you on the truck. We were put on a truck. Now, some of them have to walk. We were stuck on trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all get to go the same way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were all [INAUDIBLE] all got [INAUDIBLE] on that truck. All got on the truck, with other people, of course. It was packed like-- in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me. So you got off the train and all the bright lights were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the bright lights were there. And they [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That wagon went this way, and that wagon-- the boxcar went that way, this boxcar went that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that whole process take for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seemed like it was all night. Because we got into this camp, now we had to get down, come off those trucks again. And now you're marching in this camp business. All the lights, you really couldn't see at night, just those lights. And then they put us in those barracks. Was 800 or 1,000 people in there. And you just had to stick around there. They had a big-- like a that was built out of bricks. You used to heat that, they used to heat. It's supposed to be for heating situation. But it was well enough you could sit on it. And the children screaming and sleepy and crying. What they were doing, they were putting the numbers on us. They wouldn't put us in any blocks, until you had all this number business. So they started with the numbers first. Then the worst thing is that un-lousing thing. When we had to go through after that, you go into the un-lousing. You strip. Now comes the finale, as far as my parents are concerned. I told you how [GERMAN], how little innocent we were raised. Here's my mother with her big children there, big boys, in the nude. [SOBBING] She covered herself. All she had to say was, stay together, kids, stay together. She had the little ones in front of her. I think that killed her. That was the beginning of the end for her, this little-- whatever she had. And of course we all had to strip then of course. Everybody did. It was not just us, everyone. And then started to shave you, the hair, the arms. They spray you, you know what I mean. That un-lousing situation. That was-- I think it was the worst thing for my mom. My dad too, was terrible. I mean, anything there that broke whatever they had was finished right in there. My dad only lived-- my mother only lived three months after that. And my dad died three months after her. But my mother did get the typhoid. She didn't die from that. Anyway, that was the beginning. That's when we were-- and then the next day we was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit before.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Put into the different camps. We were put in-- oh. We were four sisters. They had picked us and put us on the side. And now my mother thought, oh my God. They're going to take my girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what one of those Nazis, those SS guys said, this was like a kitchen crew, getting lined up for kitchen crew. So we were four of us-- first they asked us. Because we looked healthy, there was no doubt about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were healthy looking girls. We were farm girls, we were used to hard work. So we looked pretty healthy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was my sister Mathilde, my sister Charlotte, my sister Lena, and me. We were picked for this crew, for this kitchen crew. But we had to go in quarantine, we couldn't go with the parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they said, the Nazi guy said, you're not leaving, you're staying here. You're just going to go into quarantine. That's number one block. And your parents are going in the number five block.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's all we could do. We had to take their word for it. And it was so. They did put us in the quarantine block. What about the little children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went with the mama. She had all the kids with her. And we didn't get to see them for six weeks. They did put us in this quarantine. Because we were not alone. We were cooking, we had to learn how to cook for those Nazis-- for the prisoners. We also had to cook for the Nazis. So they wanted make sure that part was clean.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well anyway, three of my sisters were under the kettles there with the cooking. I was in the magazine. You know what a magazine is? A magazine is where they store all the flour, the margarine, the canned stuff, whatever they were serving.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had to come on the window. It had to be weighed up. And you had to give it to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in there. And there was a Polish man in there. He was from Poland, couldn't speak German. I couldn't speak Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But pointing with each other, we got along. So I was in with the Polish guy in the magazine. That's when I was working for him. And it was about six weeks before the quarantine was called off. And we were on one week, and the weekend was on a Sunday. We were allowed-- not all of us, they took some of the girls could go on next week, the other girls could go. So I with my other sister, we would go visit up here on the block. They were in block five. And already it's six weeks later, I'd seen my dad. My dad was a big man, so like Fritz, I showed you. That's how my dad-- you could see it on the pictures. My dad was a big man. And already he was-- and then I looked at my mother. And she was sitting-- and we had two booths, because we had too many children. Couldn't have just one, we had to have two.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, two with all those people, that's the way it was. It was OK. We got used to the situation. Now if you lived on those boards. Those boards were beautiful later. It's something you adapt to, you know? So the whole family, except for the four girls, the family stayed together. Was that unusual?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was not unusual. They needed help. They got new people came in. They needed help for the kitchen. But even kitchen help gets sick, gets typhoid, out you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it unusual for the families to stay together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a lot of people got separated. We got separated afterwards. But at that time was good. We were here, we were [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, I came to see my father. My mother and I. She said, don't worry about him. He's too big anyway. He could lose a few pounds. I said, God, mom, he don't look good. Mother saw all that way, don't want to get the kids upset. And don't worry, everything going to be OK. It's going to be fine. And she said, you just make sure, she said, do your work there. And don't do anything to hurt anybody. And I went back home. And I went back to the kitchen. And I sat there in that magazine, and I just cried and cried and cried.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that Polish man came to me. And he said to me, in a broken German, he had learned a few words. He said, why are you crying? I said, well, I said what happened, what I see my--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you're lucky. You're lucky, he says, you're working. I was working there in the kitchen, I had my sisters. He said, you could be out there, working on the thing. I said, well what's the difference. If I work here or work there, it's the same thing. He said, well, sometimes it's rough out there, much rougher out there than in here. I didn't know that. But in the meantime, I did get the typhoid. My other sister got sick. So we wound up in sick blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know about typhoid? I'm sure you heard about typhoid. It's a terrible thing, terrible, terrible thing. They get those high fevers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know I had a toothache. And I went over to block to that block leader. And that block leader, kind of took my dad. My dad helped him dispense food there. So he had a little job in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to my father, he said, what has she got, a toothache? And I said, yeah, I said I got a toothache. So I said I don't know where to go. And I had a terrible toothache. And he said, well, he said, why don't you wait and I'll see what I could do? So he came over with another-- I was sitting there, waiting for him to come. He came with another block leader, from eight, from block eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said come with us, come with us. And they took me up in some near sick block there. And there were in there was a Polish guy. He was a prisoner, but he used to be a dentist.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they sat me down over there [INAUDIBLE]. They set me on the table. They're going to hold me down there.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had a terrible toothache. And what they did [INAUDIBLE]. Well, this man pulled-- I lost my first tooth there, pulled my eye tooth, with one root. And he took that tooth out. And he showed it, and there was a little sack hanging on the bottom. He said, are you lucky. He said, if that little sack would have went, there was pus in that thing. They would have went and would have killed me, right then and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here.
